narrative:
Karnataka High Court heard **271 of 685 scheduled matters**, an overall hearing rate of **39.6%** on 14 May. That is a moderate-throughput day in absolute terms, but it sits **below the 45.1%** recorded in the available prior context for **12 May**, even though total hearings were higher today (**271 vs 214**) because the listed volume was much larger (**685 vs 474**).

Judge-wise, the strongest conversion came from **H.P. Sandesh & P. Sree Sudha**, who heard **40 of 49 cases (81.6%)**, comfortably the best performance of the day. **S.R. Krishna Kumar** showed a sharp split across two listed batches: one converted **82 of 124 matters (66.1%)**, while the larger batch converted only **66 of 342 (19.3%)**, making roster composition a major factor in overall output. **M.G.S. Kamal** was mid-range at **75 of 156 (48.1%)** on the main board, with a smaller list at **8 of 14 (57.1%)**.

Stage conversion was led by **Admission** matters at **22 of 43 (51.2%)**, while **Orders** remained the largest and most important pipeline at **156 of 359 (43.5%)**. **Preliminary Hearing** underperformed at **69 of 207 (33.3%)**, a notable drop from **83.3%** in the 12 May context, suggesting weaker early-stage movement despite a heavier list. **Final Hearing** was **1 of 2**, too small to infer much.

By case type, **WP** dominated the docket with **487 scheduled cases**, but only **168 were heard (34.5%)**, which materially pulled down the court-wide rate. **CRL.P** was more efficient at **63 of 121 (52.1%)**, improving on the **40.9%** seen on 12 May. Smaller categories such as **RFA (71.4%)**, **CRL.A (63.6%)**, **WA (63.6%)**, and **WPHC (100%, 5 of 5)** converted well, but their volumes were too small to offset the writ-heavy backlog pressure.
